japanese: ポークカツレツサラダ

food_subgroup: Breaded and fried meat with salad

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a pork cutlet food product entry but no specific entry for pork cutlet salad as a composed dish; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for breaded pork cutlet salad; A0CKP (pig meat, cooked) used as closest parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for pork cutlet salad as a composed dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 300 g, consisting of one breaded and fried pork cutlet (approximately 150 g) sliced and placed over a bed of shredded cabbage and mixed salad greens (approximately 150 g), typically served with sesame dressing or tonkatsu sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Pork, fresh, loin, whole, separable lean and fat, cooked, braised (USDA SR Legacy 10062) combined with breaded coating and salad component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy) and USDA Foundation Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Pork cutlet salad is a composed dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for cooked pork loin (FDC ID 10062), panko breadcrumbs (FDC ID 172822), and raw cabbage/mixed greens (FDC ID 169975), weighted to reflect a typical 50% pork cutlet / 50% salad composition per 100 g of the finished dish. Cooking oil absorption for deep-frying estimated at approximately 8–10% of cutlet weight per standard frying absorption factors. Values represent a composite estimate and carry medium confidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork cutlet salad as a composed dish. GI of 48 is estimated from the analogous food 'breaded and fried meat with mixed salad and dressing', anchored to published GI values for panko-breaded fried foods (GI approximately 45–55, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. The salad component (cabbage, mixed greens) contributes negligible glycemic load. Composite GI weighted toward the breaded cutlet component. GL per serving = (48 × 12.8 × 300) / (100 × 100) = 18.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 310 mg per 100 g; a full serving (300 g) contributes approximately 930 mg sodium, representing about 40% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by major cardiovascular guidelines.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium dressing and limit portion size to one serving, as the combined sodium from the breaded cutlet and commercial dressing can contribute substantially to daily sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic index of 48 (low GI) but glycemic load per serving is approximately 18.4 g due to the panko breadcrumb coating; fat content from deep-frying may also slow gastric emptying and affect postprandial glucose variability.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can include pork cutlet salad occasionally given its low GI, but should monitor portion size of the breaded cutlet component and choose a low-sugar dressing to manage overall glycemic load and caloric intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-frying adds approximately 8–10% fat by weight to the cutlet, contributing saturated fat (2.6 g per 100 g) and total fat (10.2 g per 100 g); cholesterol approximately 52 mg per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption of deep-fried pork cutlet salad and consider requesting a baked or air-fried preparation to reduce saturated fat and total fat intake in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Cutlet Salad (Japanese: ポークカツレツサラダ, Pōku Katsuretsu Sarada) is a Japanese-inspired dish consisting of a deep-fried breaded pork cutlet — typically made from pork loin or pork fillet coated in panko breadcrumbs — sliced and served over a bed of shredded cabbage and mixed salad greens, usually accompanied by sesame dressing, tonkatsu sauce, or a light Japanese-style vinaigrette. The dish combines the crispy, golden exterior and juicy interior of a tonkatsu-style cutlet with the freshness and crunch of raw vegetables, creating a contrast of textures and flavours. It is commonly found in Japanese curry restaurants, Japanese teishoku (set meal) establishments, and Japanese-influenced casual dining venues across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, a 300 g serving provides approximately 585 kcal, 40.5 g protein, 30.6 g fat, and 38.4 g carbohydrate. The dish is a moderate source of B vitamins (particularly B1, B3, and B6 from pork), iron, zinc, and vitamin C from the salad component. Sodium content is moderate to high depending on the dressing used. The glycemic index is estimated at 48 (low GI), though the glycemic load per serving is approximately 18 g due to the panko coating. Clinically, the dish is suitable for occasional consumption by individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes given its low GI, but portion control and dressing choice are important. Sodium and saturated fat content warrant attention for those with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those with gluten or egg allergies.
common_user_queries: How many calories in pork cutlet salad?

confidence_reason: No exact match for pork cutlet salad as a composed dish was found in any primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for cooked pork loin, panko breadcrumbs, and raw salad greens, weighted by typical dish composition. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to published values for breaded fried meat from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source data for this food category and is not reliably estimated for a composed dish of this type. 2. Nutrient values represent a composite estimate for a 50% pork cutlet / 50% salad composition per 100 g of finished dish; actual values will vary by restaurant preparation, oil type, dressing quantity, and cutlet thickness. 3. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for pork cutlet salad was identified in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the accessed date. 4. The input food_group was listed as 'Vegetables' in the seed record; this has been corrected to 'Meat and alternatives' as the primary macronutrient and caloric contribution comes from the breaded pork cutlet component. 5. Halal status is marked false as pork is not halal; kosher status is false as pork is not kosher. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg through selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component data and carry medium confidence. 7. trans_fat_g of 0.1 reflects trace naturally occurring trans fats from pork; this is not a confirmed zero and is a plausible estimate from USDA data.
